Power and cost: three-arm persuasion experiment

Design and statistics follow Röttger et al.’s ai-distortion persuasion follow-up (Study 5), refit here to validate consistency.

1. Model and assumptions

Statistical model (identical to Paul’s persuasion_regressions_full_analyses.R):

\[\text{stance\_shift} \;\sim\; \text{arm} \;+\; (1\mid\text{reader}) \;+\; (1\mid\text{proposition})\]

a Gaussian linear mixed model (glmmTMB), crossed random intercepts for reader and proposition (no random slopes — the arm effect is taken constant across readers and propositions, as preregistered). The outcome is the oriented stance shift \(\text{direction}\times(\text{attitude}_\text{post}-\text{attitude}_\text{pre})\) on the 0–100 scale. arm \(\in\) {neutral (reference), for, against}.

Estimand and inference. The for-vs-neutral average marginal effect \(\Delta\), computed with marginaleffects::avg_comparisons (delta method, re.form = NA) — Paul’s exact procedure. An effect is detected when its 95% CI excludes 0. Reported across assumed \(\Delta \in \{1,2,5,10\}\) points.

Design. Within-participant; each participant reads 6 paragraphs — 2 per arm.

Proposition pool. 12 propositions (selection rule in §4).

Power. 90% power, two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\). The contrast SE is obtained by simulating the model above with the fitted variance components over the 12-proposition pool; it scales as \(SE(N)=se_\text{unit}/\sqrt{N_\text{readers}}\), so \(n=\big((z_{1-\alpha/2}+z_\text{power})\,se_\text{unit}/\Delta\big)^2\).

Cost model (Paul’s): £12 per rater-hour (£9 + 33% Prolific fee); 1.5 min per paragraph + 2.5 min fixed overhead per rater.

## 39,980 reader-paragraph rows | 7,996 readers | 98 propositions

2. Validation against Paul’s data

The model is refit on data/followup_persuasion. Point estimates, standard errors and 95% confidence intervals for the average marginal effects and condition means are computed with marginaleffects (delta method, re.form = NA) — Paul’s exact procedure — and compared with the values committed in results/followup_persuasion. (Paul reports CIs and z-statistics; his SE is recovered as estimate/z for the AMEs and as CI-width\(/2z_{.975}\) for the condition means.)

d1 <- df %>% filter(!is.na(stance_shift), !is.na(paragraph_type3))
m1 <- glmmTMB(stance_shift ~ paragraph_type3 + (1 | reader_id) + (1 | proposition_id),
              data = d1, family = gaussian())
d2 <- df %>% filter(!is.na(stance_shift), !is.na(condition5))
m2 <- glmmTMB(stance_shift ~ condition5 + (1 | reader_id) + (1 | proposition_id),
              data = d2, family = gaussian())
ac_ref  <- avg_comparisons(m1, variables = list(paragraph_type3 = "reference"),
                           type = "response", re.form = NA)
ac_pair <- avg_comparisons(m1, variables = list(paragraph_type3 = c("human", "ai")),
                           type = "response", re.form = NA)
preds   <- avg_predictions(m2, by = "condition5", type = "response", re.form = NA)
pick <- function(tab, ...) {
  hits <- Reduce(`&`, lapply(c(...), function(s) grepl(s, tab$contrast))); tab[hits, ]
}
mine <- bind_rows(
  tibble(quantity = c("AME: AI vs control", "AME: human vs control", "AME: AI vs human"),
         estimate = c(pick(ac_ref, "ai", "control")$estimate,
                      pick(ac_ref, "human", "control")$estimate, ac_pair$estimate),
         se       = c(pick(ac_ref, "ai", "control")$std.error,
                      pick(ac_ref, "human", "control")$std.error, ac_pair$std.error),
         ci_low   = c(pick(ac_ref, "ai", "control")$conf.low,
                      pick(ac_ref, "human", "control")$conf.low, ac_pair$conf.low),
         ci_high  = c(pick(ac_ref, "ai", "control")$conf.high,
                      pick(ac_ref, "human", "control")$conf.high, ac_pair$conf.high)),
  tibble(quantity = paste0("mean shift: ", as.character(preds$condition5)),
         estimate = preds$estimate, se = preds$std.error,
         ci_low = preds$conf.low, ci_high = preds$conf.high)
)

ame_c <- read_csv(file.path(RESULTS, "rq1_ame.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
cm_c  <- read_csv(file.path(RESULTS, "rq2_condition_means.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
Z <- qnorm(0.975)
get_ame <- function(term) ame_c[ame_c$term == term, ]
committed <- bind_rows(
  tibble(quantity = c("AME: AI vs control", "AME: human vs control", "AME: AI vs human"),
         paul_est = c(get_ame("paragraph_type3_ai_vs_control")$ame,
                      get_ame("paragraph_type3_human_vs_control")$ame,
                      get_ame("paragraph_type_ai_vs_human")$ame),
         paul_lo  = c(get_ame("paragraph_type3_ai_vs_control")$ame_low,
                      get_ame("paragraph_type3_human_vs_control")$ame_low,
                      get_ame("paragraph_type_ai_vs_human")$ame_low),
         paul_hi  = c(get_ame("paragraph_type3_ai_vs_control")$ame_high,
                      get_ame("paragraph_type3_human_vs_control")$ame_high,
                      get_ame("paragraph_type_ai_vs_human")$ame_high)),
  tibble(quantity = paste0("mean shift: ", cm_c$condition),
         paul_est = cm_c$mean_stance_shift, paul_lo = cm_c$conf.low, paul_hi = cm_c$conf.high)
) %>% mutate(paul_se = (paul_hi - paul_lo) / (2 * Z))

out <- mine %>% left_join(committed, by = "quantity") %>%
  mutate(`|d est|` = abs(estimate - paul_est),
         `|d SE|`  = abs(se - paul_se),
         `|d CI|`  = pmax(abs(ci_low - paul_lo), abs(ci_high - paul_hi)))
kable(out %>% select(quantity, estimate, se, ci_low, ci_high, paul_est, paul_se,
                     `|d est|`, `|d SE|`, `|d CI|`), digits = 4)
quantity estimate se ci_low ci_high paul_est paul_se |d est| |d SE| |d CI|
AME: AI vs control 4.3254 0.2180 3.8980 4.7528 4.3254 0.2180 0 0.0000 0.0000
AME: human vs control 2.9127 0.2180 2.4854 3.3400 2.9127 0.2180 0 0.0000 0.0000
AME: AI vs human 1.4127 0.1780 1.0639 1.7616 1.4127 0.1780 0 0.0000 0.0000
mean shift: control -0.0128 0.2684 -0.5389 0.5133 -0.0128 0.2684 0 0.0000 0.0000
mean shift: human-low 4.4489 0.2709 3.9179 4.9799 4.4489 0.2715 0 0.0006 0.0011
mean shift: human-high 1.3491 0.2708 0.8183 1.8799 1.3491 0.2706 0 0.0003 0.0005
mean shift: ai-low 4.4098 0.2706 3.8794 4.9401 4.4098 0.2722 0 0.0016 0.0032
mean shift: ai-high 4.2148 0.2700 3.6855 4.7440 4.2148 0.2698 0 0.0003 0.0005

Condition means with Paul’s model 95% CIs (avg_predictions, re.form = NA):

Mean oriented stance shift by presentation position (positions randomised in Paul’s data):

order mean sd n
1 3.56 18.88 6347
2 3.29 17.17 6411
3 3.59 16.77 6417
4 3.75 16.18 6363
5 3.62 16.12 6446

3. Variance components and the contrast SE

vc <- VarCorr(m2)$cond
varcomp <- list(reader_sd = as.numeric(attr(vc$reader_id, "stddev")),
                prop_sd   = as.numeric(attr(vc$proposition_id, "stddev")),
                resid_sd  = sigma(m2))
tibble(component = c("reader", "proposition", "residual", "marginal"),
       sd = c(varcomp$reader_sd, varcomp$prop_sd, varcomp$resid_sd,
              sqrt(varcomp$reader_sd^2 + varcomp$prop_sd^2 + varcomp$resid_sd^2))) %>%
  kable(digits = 3)
component sd
reader 2.937
proposition 1.924
residual 15.866
marginal 16.250 
set.seed(1)
su <- simulate_ame_se_unit(varcomp, N = 1200, reps = 5, pool = 12, qpa = 2)
SE_UNIT <- su$se_unit
cat(sprintf("se_unit (for-vs-neutral AME SE * sqrt(N)) = %.2f\n", SE_UNIT))
## se_unit (for-vs-neutral AME SE * sqrt(N)) = 15.84

The simulated for-vs-neutral AME SE equals the armfor coefficient SE (identical to \(10^{-6}\)), so the uncertainty driving the power calculation is computed exactly as in §2. A direct check: at the N predicted for 90% power at \(\Delta = 3\) (N \(\approx\) 294), 200 simulated experiments have the AME 95% CI exclude 0 in 92% of cases.

4. Proposition selection

Rule: the 6 left-coded and 6 right-coded propositions with the lowest mean writer stance-extremity (mean \(|\text{writer\_stance\_pre}-50|\) from the main study), 12 total. Realised persuadability (mean oriented shift in Paul’s follow-up) shown for reference.

feats <- proposition_features(DATA)
y <- persuadability(df)
kable(select_propositions(feats, y, n_per_side = 6), digits = 2)
proposition_id proposition_leaning w_extremity w_conf n_writers persuadability
248 left 21.53 54.56 45 0.91
346 left 22.09 63.02 44 6.51
353 left 22.22 60.38 45 4.80
82 left 22.26 59.57 46 6.67
390 left 22.63 67.93 46 3.31
356 left 22.76 66.22 46 2.39
184 right 16.93 63.76 45 4.54
230 right 18.11 65.76 45 4.62
187 right 18.98 64.33 45 3.99
342 right 20.84 68.11 45 4.75
377 right 22.43 70.84 44 5.84
374 right 22.49 63.84 45 3.80

5. Power and cost

Participants and total cost to detect a biased-vs-neutral AME \(\Delta\) at 90% power (two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\)), Design A (6 questions, 2 per arm).

kable(cost_table(deltas = c(1, 2, 5, 10), se_unit = SE_UNIT, power = 0.90) %>%
        select(delta, n_raters, questions_per_rater, rater_hours, total_cost_gbp))
delta n_raters questions_per_rater rater_hours total_cost_gbp
1 2638 6 506 6067
2 660 6 126 1518
5 106 6 20 244
10 27 6 5 62

6. Minimum detectable effect at a fixed budget

Smallest biased-vs-neutral AME detectable at 90% power for £400 and £800 budgets, Design A.

kable(mde_table(budgets = c(400, 800), se_unit = SE_UNIT, power = 0.90))
budget_gbp n_raters mde_pts
400 173 3.89
800 347 2.75
